---
title: "Drawing Democracies: Redistricting in America"
format:
  harvard-diss-typst:
    keep-typ: true
author:
  - name: Christopher T. Kenny
    affiliations:
      - name: Harvard University
        department: The Department of Government
        city: Cambridge, Massachusetts
bibliography: bibliography.bib
dissertation-advisor: "Professor Kosuke Imai"
degree-subject: Government
date: 2025-04-25
abstract: |
  Write an abstract here which displays the contents that you want.
  More often than not, this will take a bit over a page.
  There are Quarto extensions which allow to write the abstract as normal text, rather than YAML.
thanks: |
  It is customary to include some form of acknowledgements to thank those who made this possible.
dedication: To Coda and Tibble, without whom this dissertation would never have been completed.
epigraph: |
  | Redistricting is like an election in reverse.
  | It's a great event.
  | Usually the voters get to pick the politicians.
  | In redistricting, the politicians get to pick the voters.
epigraph-author: "Thomas Hofeller"
list-figures: true
list-tables: true
previous-work: |
  @sec-more-info is ... (note here that you can use cross references like at the start of this line even though we are in the YAML)

  Chapter 2 is drawn from @mccartan2024redistricting.
---

## Introduction {.introduction}

This is a template for Harvard GSAS theses.
It follows the guidelines in <https://gsas.harvard.edu/policy/formatting-your-dissertation> and <https://gsas.harvard.edu/resource/dissertation-formatting-guidance>, as of 2025-05-01.

Read the readme for the full instructions for use.

## More Information on Chapters {#sec-more-info}

Note that this is set up to call each "section" a chapter and so references look like @sec-more-info.

I recommend using two `##` signs for chapters.
In theory, you can use one `#` and it should detect this and work nicely.

You likely want to use the include shortcodes to include your chapters.
See <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/includes.html> for a description of how to do exactly that.
However, *all* references do need to be combined into one bibtex, hayagriva, or equivalent file currently.

### A subsection

This should be within a level 2 heading.

![A figure. This follows the behavior of the first sentence being the short title/description, like in general science journals. The first sentence will be used to identify the figure in the TOC for figures, if enabled.]({{< placeholder 400 200 format=svg >}}){#fig-example}

See @sec-more-info for more info.

## Appendix for Chapter 1 {.appendix #sec-app-1}

Some text to supplement @sec-more-info.

![An appendix figure]({{< placeholder 400 200 format=svg >}}){#fig-appx-example}
